```java 
public class Try {  
    void abc() {} // old name for this function/method, now named as 'kerb' or whatever you want.    	
	void ref(){      			// This method is calling the new renamed one (abc).     			        										}        }`  public static `void main(String[] args) {   Try t =new T;   	t .ref(); }} // Calling a function named 'kerbCrawling' in class.
```	In this code, the method "abc" is renamed to kerbCrawling and it does nothing at present (because there are no statements inside). The ref() calling abc(), so when you call t .ref(); , Java will execute function named 'kerbCrawling' in class.

Extract a temporary variable from the switch expression and name it kerbCrawling:
content:
In Java, you can't directly assign an enum value to a temporary variable in the switch statement. However, we could use reflection or getter methods for this purpose if it is possible within your context (like inside another method). Here I will provide two ways using both of them and explain why they are used:

1) Using Reflection - This approach requires you have access to all enum values in the code. 
```java
enum Bug { X } // assuming this was defined somewhere else, it's not possible here without reflection or getter methods for each value of bug type (X Y Z etc.)  
Bug kerbCrawling = null;   
try{kerbCrawling=(Bug)Enum.valueOf(Class.forName("YourPackage"+ Bug._YOUR_ENUM_.getName()), "KERBCRAWLING");}catch (Exception e){e.printStackTrace();};  // replace YourPackage and Kerbcraving with your actual package name & enum value
```   In the above code, `YourPackage` should be replaced by a real Java Package Name where Bug Enum is defined in that class file(like com/example or similar) ,  `KERBCRAWLING`  replace it to whatever you want. This approach can work if all enum values are known at compile time and their names match exactly with the case of your switch statement (case "X", etc).
    If not, then we need a way for our code to get these information dynamically or use reflection in some other ways like below: 
    ```java  
      Bug kerbCrawling = null; // assuming this was defined somewhere else. It's impossible here without using Reflection and getting the enum values at runtime (like inside another method).    Enum[] allEnums=Bug.class.getEnumConstants();  for(int i = 0 ;i<allEnums .length,++){   if("X".equalsIgnoreCase(((Bug)allEnums[i]).name())){kerbCrawling = (Bug )allEnums [i]; break;} }
    ```    In the above code `getEnumConstants()` returns all enum constants of Bug and then we are checking if name matches with your case. If it does, assign that value to kerbCrawling variable else continue looping until you find a match or reach end of array (if no matching found).
    Please note: Reflection can be slower than other methods because reflection requires loading the class and its constants into memory at runtime which is not possible in switch case.  Also, it's generally considered bad practice to use this approach as Java enums are meant for compile-time checking of enum values (like above code), so using them directly inside a `switch` statement can be more readable or maintainable than reflection and could lead you into an infinite loop if not handled properly.

Extract a class as an enum and name it kerbCrawling:
content:
In Java, you can use the `enum` keyword to create an enumerated type. Here's how your code would look like if I understand correctly what you want from it (i.e., a class that represents different types of kerb crawling):

```java
public enum KerbCrawling {  // name the new Enum as per Java naming conventions, usually use PascalCase for enums in .NET languages like C# or JavaScript etc...  
    LEFT(0), RIGHT(1), CENTERED(2), JUSTIFIED(3);     // define constants here.  You can also add methods to the enum if needed (like a method that returns all possible values).     
      
    private final int value;                          // declare an instance variable for each constant, and initialize it with its ordinal position in this Enum type...  
                                                      // ...and assign them as per your requirement.  The constructor is used to set the initial state of these variables when a new object gets created from enum class..   
     KerbCrawling(int value) {                       // define private variable for each constant, and initialize it with its ordinal position in this Enum type...  
        this.value = value;                          // ...and assign them as per your requirement when a new object gets created from enum class..   
     }                                                 
                                                                  public int getValue() { return value;}      /* add method to retrieve the constant's integer */      
}                     
```  
In this code, `KerbCrawling` is an enumerated type that represents different types of kerbing. The constants are defined as fields in your class and can be accessed directly using their names (e.g., KerbCrawling.LEFT). You also have the option to add methods if needed, like a method returning all possible values or something similar based on what you need for this enumerated type's use case(like getting value of each constant in enum class)

content:
Here is how you can add and remove courses from the list using methods named `addkerbCrawling` for adding course, and `removeKerbCrowing` to delete one or more specific items in your collection. This example assumes that a Course object represents each individual item on our List (i.e., it has name attribute).
```java 
public class Person {  
    private ArrayList<String> courses; // Use 'List' instead of Arraylist for better performance and easier manipulation later if needed, but this is just an example to show how you can use them both in one program    
      
      public void addkerbCrawling(ArrayList < String > newcourse) { 
        System.out .println("Adding courses: " + Arrays.toString (newcourses)); // Prints the added course names for verification  
          this.courses = new ArrayList<String>();    }     public void removekerbCrawling(ArrayList < String > delcourse) {  System .out .println ("Removing courses: " + Arrays..toString (delcourse)); // Prints the removed course names for verification  
          this.courses = new ArrayList<String>();    }     public static void main() {}      /*public Person(ArrayList < String > initialCourse) {  super () ;this .initializeCollection ("initial courses", Arrays..toString (Arrays.));}*/ // Constructor to initialize the collection with some values  
          private ArrayList<String> getInitialcourses(){return new Arraylist<>();}}    /*public void addkerbCrawling(ArrayList < String > course) {this.getinitialcourse().addAll((List )Arrays..toString (new array));}*/ // Adds all courses to the list  
          public staticvoid main() {}  private ArrayList<String> getInitialcourses(){return new Arraylist<>();}}    /*public void removekerbCrawling(ArrayList < String > course) {this.getinitialcourse().removeAll((List )Arrays..toString (new array));}*/ // Removes all courses from the list  
          public staticvoid main() {}  }     */``` This is a simple example and might not cover your exact use case, but it should give you an idea of how to add/delete items in collections. Please adjust as per requirements! Also note that this code assumes Java version >=8 for `Arrays` methods (used here).

prompt: Encapsulate the record by adding methods to get and set names. Name them getkerbCrawling and setkerbCrawling:
content:
Sure, here is how you can encapsulate the record by adding methods to set and get names. Name them `getkerbCrawling` for getting name and `setKerbCrowing` or any other suitable method in your class's body as setting a new value of 'name':
```java 
public String organization; // Declare the variable inside public so it can be accessed from outside. It is not recommended to use private here, but for simplicity I am using this way due to lack of context about what you want in your class's body and how they should interact with each other (getter/setters).
 
public String getOrganization() { // Get method declaration - returns the value stored inside organization. This is called a 'Getter'. It can be used outside this scope to access or print out values of variables in your class, but not directly modify them as they are private and should only use methods like setName(String name)
    return "Organization{name='" + organization  + "'";  // This is the complete string that you want. It includes 'Acme Gooseberries' inside a double quote which marks it to be treated just once, not as part of another word or phrase (like in setName). If your actual name was stored elsewhere and this line only prints out its value then there would need to be more code here for that too
}  // End getter method declaration. This is the end statement after a return keyword which indicates when you are done with what's inside parentheses of function (in our case, it ends up being returning organization name). It must always have an ending semicolon in Java as per language rules and syntax
  
public void setOrganization(String newName) { // Set method declaration - this is used to change the value stored within 'organization' variable. This can be called from outside of class, passing it a parameter (new name). It must always have an ending semicolon in Java as per language rules and syntax
    organization = "Acme Gooseberries"; // Here we are setting our newName to your actual value or whatever you want the 'organization' variable set at. This is done inside parentheses of method (in this case, it takes a parameter called `new name`). If there were more code here then that would be where those changes will take place
}  // End Setter Method Declaration - end statement for setting new value to organization and ending with semicolon. It must always have an ending semi-colon in Java as per language rules, syntax & conventions of the programming languages you are using (Java is one such example). This method can be used inside or outside class body
``` 
This way we encapsulate our data by making it accessible only through methods and providing a mechanism to change its value. It's not recommended for public variables, but I have done this in your case due lack of context about what you want the 'name' variable should do with inside or outside class body (getter/setters).

Extract a superclass and name it kerbCrawling:
content:
In Java, a class can extend another by using the extends keyword followed by its parent's name and optionally implement interfaces with an implements keyword separated from each other by commas (if any). Here is how you could create your superclass named kerbCrawling. 

```java
public abstract class KerbCrawling { // Declare the Abstract Class here, it can't be instantiated directly like a normal Java classes but only its subclasses will extend this one and implement interfaces or declare methods in an interface (like FilterReader) that are declared by superclass. 
    public final Reader reader;   // Assigning type of field to the variable is not necessary, it's just for demonstration purpose here as per your code snippet provided above which declares a char array cbuf and assign its length in read method from FilterReader class (which extends KerbCrawling).
    
    public abstract int getReadLimit(); // Declare the methods that will be implemented by subclasses. 
  
}
```
Then, you can extend your ClassA with this new superclass and implement any interface or method declared in FilterReader:

```java
public class MyClass extends KerbCrawling {   
     public int getReadLimit() { return -1; } // Implement the abstract methods from parent's classes. 
}  
 ```     
This way, you can have a superclass that has common features and subclasses which implement those same or different set of behaviors (methods). This is an example to show how inheritance in Java works with interfaces as well! You could also add more methods if needed within your KerbCrawling class. 
Remember: Abstract classes can't be instantiated directly, they must have at least one abstract method that needs implementation by any subclass (or super-subclasses). If you want to create an instance of a ClassA or use the FilterReader methods in this way then it is not possible because these are both final and cannot change.

content:
Sure, I can help with that! Here's how you could separate the query from its modifier and name it `kerbCrawling` in Java method format as follows: 
```java
public double kerbCrawling() { // Method declaration.
    double result = 0;          // Variable initialization for accumulating total outstanding amount of invoices.
    
    List<Invoice> customerInvoices = this.customer.getInvoices();  // Querying the list/collection 'invoices' from a Customer object in your class, assuming it is accessible and populated correctly here (assuming you have access to getter methods for these).  
    
    if(Objects.nonNull(customerInvoices) && ! customerInvoices .isEmpty()){  // Checking whether the list/collection 'invoices' from a Customer object is not null and has at least one element before proceeding with loop iteration (assuming you have access to these methods).
        for (Invoice each : customer.getInvoices()) {   // Loop through all invoices in your collection or list of Invoices, assuming it's accessible here as well. 
            result += each . getAmount();    // Modifying the 'result', adding up total outstanding amount for every Invoice object (assuming you have access to these methods).  
        }    
         sendBill() ;// Calling a method named `sendBill`, assuming it's accessible here.  This will be responsible of sending bill after all invoices are processed and total outstanding amount is calculated above loop iteration (assuming you have access to this function).   // End if block
    }    
      return result;// Returning the final value which includes summed up values from each Invoice object.  Assumed by your code that `sendBill` method will be called after all invoices are processed and total outstanding amount is calculated above loop iteration (assuming you have access to this function).
} // End of Method declaration  ```java   ^ This should give a good starting point for the kerbCrawling.  Please adjust as per your actual class structure, variable names etc! Make sure all necessary imports are included in each method and block accordingly (assuming you have access to them).

Split the variable and use a variable for the temp and name it kerbCrawling:
content:
In your code, you're trying to calculate the area of a rectangle and then print it twice using two different variables (`kerbCrawling1` & `kerbCrawling2`). However, in Java variable names cannot be reused as they are scoped differently. 

If we want your code into one line with temporary storage for the calculation of area:
```java
int height = 5; // or any other value you need to calculate it dynamically later on if needed...  
double width = (height / Math.sqrt(2));// assuming square root is used here, otherwise use a constant like 3 in your case..   
System.out.println("Area: " + height * ((int)width + 10/((4*Math.tan(6))))); // you may need to adjust the formula based on what exactly needs it...  
```
This code will calculate and print out area of a rectangle with given `height`, but not reuse any variable for temporary storage (like in your original question).  If we want this calculation into one line using two different variables then you can't do that because Java is statically typed language. You need to declare both the temp1 and kerbCrawling at once like:
```java  
int height = 5; // or any other value...   
double width = (height / Math.sqrt(2));// assuming square root used here, otherwise use a constant.. 
System.out.println("Area is " + ((width + 10/((4*Math.tan(6))))) * height);  ```     This will print out the area of rectangle with given `height` and reuse temp variable for temporary storage in one line as per your requirement, but not to be used again later on if needed...
